common_aliases: SLS, Sodium Laurilsulfate, Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate
explanation: Harsh surfactant that strips the skin's natural oils and disrupts the skin barrier. One of the most irritating cleansing agents used in cosmetics.

common_aliases: SLES, Sodium Lauryl Ether Sulfate, Sodium Polyoxyethylene Lauryl Ether Sulfate
explanation: Milder than SLS but still a strong surfactant. Can cause irritation with prolonged use, especially in sensitive individuals.
Similar to SLS in irritation potential. Strips natural oils and can cause dryness and redness.
Slightly milder than ALS. Still a strong cleanser that can disrupt the skin barrier over time.
Derived from coconut oil but still a harsh sulfate. Often marketed as natural but can be irritating.
Surfactant used in cleansers. Moderately irritating and can cause dryness with frequent use.
Used as a solubiliser and viscosity agent. Can be irritating at higher concentrations.
Strong surfactant with irritation potential. TEA compounds can form nitrosamines which are a safety concern.
Strong cleansing agent that can strip moisture and cause irritation in sensitive individuals.
One of the gentlest sulfate-based surfactants. Much milder than SLS or SLES. Generally well tolerated.
Derived from coconut and palm oils. Gentler than SLS and less likely to cause irritation.
Very mild sulfate-free surfactant derived from coconut oil. Gentle enough for sensitive and baby skin.
Most commonly used paraben preservative. Concerns exist around weak estrogenic activity and potential endocrine disruption, though evidence is debated.
Stronger estrogenic activity than methylparaben. More concerning from an endocrine disruption standpoint.
Has the strongest estrogenic activity among common parabens. EU has restricted its use in cosmetics for children under 3.
Moderate estrogenic activity. Less studied than methyl and propylparaben but shares similar concerns.
Strong estrogenic activity similar to butylparaben. Restricted in EU for leave-on products.
Restricted in EU cosmetics. Significant endocrine disruption concerns.
Least commonly used paraben but carries significant sensitisation risk alongside endocrine concerns.
Sodium salt form of methylparaben. Same concerns as methylparaben apply.
Sodium salt form of propylparaben. Same estrogenic activity concerns as propylparaben.
Sodium salt form of butylparaben. Carries the same endocrine disruption concerns.
One of the most highly comedogenic ingredients in cosmetics. Clogs pores rapidly and is a well-documented cause of acne cosmetica.
Highly comedogenic ester similar to isopropyl myristate. Frequently causes breakouts in acne-prone individuals.
Highly comedogenic emollient. Known to cause clogged pores and milia.
Rates 4 out of 5 on the comedogenicity scale. Despite its popularity, it clogs pores significantly for acne-prone skin.

common_aliases: Theobroma Cacao Seed Butter, Theobroma Cacao Butter, Theobroma Cocoa Butter, Cupuacu Butter, Cocoa Seed Butter
explanation: Highly comedogenic emollient. Highly occlusive and rates 4 on the comedogenicity scale. Great for dry body skin but problematic on the face.
One of the most comedogenic oils available, rating 5 out of 5. Avoid entirely if acne-prone.

common_aliases: Wool Wax, Wool Grease, Adeps Lanae
explanation: Derived from sheep wool. Rates 4 on comedogenicity. Also a known allergen for some individuals.

common_aliases: Flaxseed Oil, Linum Usitatissimum Seed Oil
explanation: Rates 4 on comedogenicity scale. Oxidises quickly which can also cause irritation.
Moderately comedogenic. Also raises environmental concerns around deforestation.
Rates 3-4 on comedogenicity. Can clog pores with regular facial use.
Rates 4 on comedogenicity. Light texture but still pore-clogging for acne-prone skin.
Rates 2-3 on comedogenicity. Borderline â€” some acne-prone individuals tolerate it, others do not.
Rates 3 on comedogenicity. Rich and heavy â€” better suited for dry skin and body use.
Rates 2-3 on comedogenicity. Despite being natural, it can disrupt the skin barrier and clog pores.
Rates 3 on comedogenicity. Frequently used in formulations but problematic for breakout-prone skin.
Rates 3-4 on comedogenicity. Common in food-grade cosmetics but clogs pores.
Modified lanolin that rates even higher on the comedogenicity scale than regular lanolin.
Rates 5 out of 5 on comedogenicity. One of the worst offenders for pore clogging.
The main fatty acid in coconut oil responsible for its comedogenicity. Rates 4 out of 5.
Saturated fatty acid that rates 3 on the comedogenicity scale.
Rates 2-3 on comedogenicity. Used widely as an emollient and emulsifier. Borderline for acne-prone skin.
Soap form of stearic acid. Can be drying and comedogenic.
Rates 3 on comedogenicity. Commonly used in lip products and foundation.
Rates 5 out of 5. Extremely comedogenic emollient used in creams and lipsticks.
Rates 4 on comedogenicity. Used as an emollient in many lotions.
Rates 3-4 on comedogenicity. Lightweight feel but still pore-clogging.
Rates 5 out of 5. Highly comedogenic emollient. Major acne trigger.
Rates 4 on comedogenicity. Common in foundations and sunscreens. Problematic for acne-prone skin.
Rates 2-3 on comedogenicity. Mild emulsifier â€” borderline for sensitive acne-prone skin.
Rates 3 on comedogenicity. Used in moisturisers and hair products.
Rates 3-4 on comedogenicity. Often found in hair pomades and heavy creams.
Technically a liquid wax, not an oil. Rates 0-2 on comedogenicity. Generally non-comedogenic and well tolerated.

common_aliases: Rosa Canina Fruit Oil, Rosa Rubiginosa Oil
explanation: Rates 1 on comedogenicity. Rich in linoleic acid which can actually help acne-prone skin. Generally safe.
Rates 0 on comedogenicity. High in linoleic acid. Non-pore-clogging and beneficial for acne-prone skin.
Rates 0-1 on comedogenicity. Lightweight and non-greasy. Generally safe for most skin types.

common_aliases: Sclerocarya Birrea Seed Oil, Sclerocarya Birrea (Marula) Seed Oil, Marula Seed Oil
explanation: Lightweight oil rich in oleic acid and antioxidants. Deeply moisturising and absorbs quickly. Can clog pores for acne-prone skin due to high oleic acid content.

common_aliases: Butyrospermum Parkii (Shea) Butter, Butyrospermum Parkii Butter, Shea Butter Extract
explanation: Rates 0-2 on comedogenicity. Generally safe but can cause issues in some acne-prone individuals. Better for body than face.
Rates 1 on comedogenicity but is extremely thick and occlusive. Can cause congestion with heavy use.
Rates 0-2 on comedogenicity. High in linoleic acid. Generally beneficial for acne-prone and sensitive skin.
Rates 1 on comedogenicity. Lightweight and non-greasy. Safe for most skin types.
Rates 2 on comedogenicity. Extremely high in carotenoids â€” can temporarily tint skin orange with prolonged use.
Rates 2 on comedogenicity. Contains zinc which can help with acne. Generally well tolerated.
Rates 2-3 on comedogenicity. Has antibacterial properties but can clog pores in acne-prone individuals.

common_aliases: Paraffinum Liquidum, White Mineral Oil, Petrolatum Liquid, Liquid Paraffin, White Petrolatum, Liquid Petrolatum, Huile Minerale, Paraffine, Nujol, Adepsine Oil
explanation: Rates 0-2 on comedogenicity. Contrary to popular belief, cosmetic grade mineral oil is not strongly comedogenic but forms a heavy occlusive layer.
Rates 0-1 on comedogenicity. Excellent occlusive moisturiser but very heavy. Better for dry skin and body use.
Rates 3 on comedogenicity. Used as an emulsifier in creams.
Rates 4-5 on comedogenicity depending on species. Despite being marketed as beneficial, many algae extracts heavily clog pores.
Derived from red algae. Rates 4 on comedogenicity. Also linked to gut inflammation when ingested.

common_aliases: D&C Red No. 17, D&C Red No. 21, D&C Red No. 3
explanation: Synthetic dyes rated 3-5 on comedogenicity. Commonly found in lip products and blush.
High concentrations can be comedogenic. Used as a thickener in shampoos and cleansers. Can cause scalp acne.
Similar to sodium chloride. Comedogenic at higher concentrations.
Rates 2 on comedogenicity. Occlusive ingredient used in lip balms and heavy creams. Can trap debris in pores.
Rates 1-2 on comedogenicity. Used in lip products and mascara. Generally tolerated but can cause issues for sensitive acne-prone skin.
Rates 1 on comedogenicity. Vegan alternative to beeswax. Generally safe for most skin types.
The single biggest cause of cosmetic allergies and contact dermatitis. A fragrance listing can hide hundreds of undisclosed chemicals. Always a red flag for sensitive skin.

common_aliases: Denatured Alcohol, SD Alcohol, Ethanol, Isopropyl Alcohol
explanation: Drying alcohol that disrupts the skin barrier. Evaporates quickly giving a fresh feel but damages the barrier over time. Different from fatty alcohols.

common_aliases: Hamamelis Virginiana Extract, Hamamelis Water, Witch Hazel Extract
explanation: Often used as a natural toner. Contains tannins that can be very drying and irritating, especially in alcohol-based formulations.
Creates a cooling sensation but is a known irritant. Can trigger flushing and worsen rosacea. Disrupts the skin barrier.
Contains high levels of menthol and menthone. Strong irritant and sensitiser. No benefit to skin despite popular use.

common_aliases: Eucalyptus Globulus Leaf Oil, Eucalyptus Essential Oil
explanation: Strong irritant with potential for sensitisation. Can trigger allergic reactions with repeated exposure.
Phototoxic â€” reacts with UV light to cause dark spots and burns. Strong irritant and sensitiser.
Highly phototoxic citrus oil. Can cause severe hyperpigmentation when skin is exposed to sunlight after use.
Contains bergapten, a known phototoxin. Can cause severe pigmentation and burns in sun-exposed skin.

common_aliases: Cinnamomum Zeylanicum Bark Extract, Cinnamon Bark Oil
explanation: One of the most common cosmetic sensitisers. Causes contact dermatitis and can permanently sensitise skin.
Eugenol from clove is a major allergen. Causes significant irritation and sensitisation.
Antibacterial properties but a known sensitiser with repeated use. Oxidises quickly and becomes more irritating over time.
Despite its calming reputation, lavender oil is a common sensitiser. Contains linalool and linalyl acetate which are allergens.
Can be irritating especially for sensitive skin. Contains camphor which is a known irritant.
Known skin sensitiser. Contains citronellol and geraniol which are common fragrance allergens.
One of the most allergenic essential oils in cosmetics. Strong sensitiser with little to no skin benefit.
Common fragrance allergen. Can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals.
Restricted by EU cosmetic regulations due to high allergenicity. Strong sensitiser.
EU restricted fragrance ingredient. Known sensitiser and allergen.
Fragrance component found in lavender and many other botanicals. One of the 26 EU-regulated fragrance allergens.
Citrus-derived fragrance component. Oxidises rapidly to form sensitising compounds. One of the 26 EU-regulated fragrance allergens.
Fragrance allergen found in rose and geranium oils. EU-regulated allergen that must be listed on labels.
Fragrance allergen found in geranium and rose oils. EU-regulated. Can cause allergic contact dermatitis.
Found in clove and cinnamon. Strong sensitiser and one of the most common contact allergens in cosmetics.
Major allergen in cinnamon. Causes contact dermatitis even in small amounts. EU-regulated fragrance allergen.
Fragrance allergen related to eugenol. Strong sensitiser â€” EU-regulated and restricted in many products.
Used as a preservative and fragrance ingredient. Can cause contact sensitivity. Also a mild irritant in higher concentrations.
Fragrance ingredient and insecticide. EU-regulated fragrance allergen. Can cause sensitisation.
Fragrance allergen found in balsam of Peru. EU-regulated. Common cause of cosmetic allergies.
Known carcinogen and strong allergen. Banned or restricted in cosmetics in many countries. Can cause severe contact dermatitis.
Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Releases formaldehyde slowly into the formula over time. Linked to hair loss in shampoos.
Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. One of the most common causes of cosmetic contact dermatitis.
Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Releases more formaldehyde than imidazolidinyl urea. Significant sensitisation risk.
Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. One of the strongest formaldehyde releasers used in cosmetics.
Preservative that releases formaldehyde. Also a potential nitrosamine former. Linked to contact dermatitis.
Powerful preservative associated with a widespread outbreak of contact allergy in Europe. EU has banned it from leave-on products.
Even more potent sensitiser than MIT. Restricted in EU rinse-off products and banned from leave-on products.
Preservative and surfactant that disrupts the skin barrier. Common in eye drops but can damage the ocular surface with prolonged use.
Used to adjust pH. At high concentrations it is severely caustic. Safe in finished products where it is neutralised, but worth noting.
Used to adjust pH similar to sodium hydroxide. Safe in neutralised finished products.
pH adjuster that can form carcinogenic nitrosamines when combined with nitrosating agents. Mild irritant and sensitiser.
Can form carcinogenic nitrosamines. EU restricts its use. Associated with liver cancer in animal studies.
Used in hair dyes and some acne treatments. Potential endocrine disruptor. Can cause sensitisation and systemic toxicity at high doses.
Skin lightening agent banned for OTC use in EU and some Asian countries. Risk of ochronosis (permanent skin darkening) with prolonged use. Requires prescription in many regions.
Antimicrobial agent banned from hand soaps in the US and restricted in EU. Potential endocrine disruptor. Contributes to antibiotic resistance.
Similar to triclosan. Banned from OTC antiseptics by FDA. Endocrine disruption concerns.
Antioxidant preservative. Listed as a possible human carcinogen by IARC. Potential endocrine disruptor.
Synthetic antioxidant. Some evidence of endocrine disruption. Considered safe at low cosmetic concentrations but debated.
Chemical UV filter. Significant skin penetration and potential endocrine disruption. Banned in Hawaii due to coral reef damage.
Chemical UV filter that penetrates skin and is found in blood and breast milk. Potential hormone disruption. Hawaii banned.
Chemical UV filter that accumulates in the body faster than it can be eliminated. EU has reduced maximum allowable concentration.
Chemical UV filter and potential endocrine disruptor. Often used to stabilise avobenzone.
Chemical UV filter with some penetration concerns. Degrades in sunlight and needs stabilisers. Generally considered safer than oxybenzone.
Derived from coal processing. Known carcinogen. Used in some dandruff and psoriasis treatments. Long-term use raises cancer risk.
Neurotoxic heavy metal compound. Used in some hair dyes. Banned in EU cosmetics.
Neurotoxic heavy metal. Found in some skin lightening products. Banned in cosmetics in most countries.
Known carcinogen. Sometimes found as contamination in natural ingredient-derived products.

common_aliases: Cd, Cadmium sulfide (CdS), Cadmium oxide (CdO), Cadmium selenide
explanation: Toxic heavy metal occasionally found as contamination in pigments and colour cosmetics.
Hexavalent chromium is a known carcinogen. Found as contamination in some colour cosmetics.

common_aliases: DBP, DEHP, DEP, Diethyl Phthalate, Dibutyl Phthalate
explanation: Group of plasticisers used in fragrances and nail products. Strong endocrine disruptors linked to reproductive harm.
Solvent found in nail polish. Neurotoxic with inhalation. Reproductive toxicant. Banned in EU nail products.
Used in nail polishes. Strong allergen and potential carcinogen. Causes contact dermatitis of the eyelids from nail touching.
Potential carcinogen found in some fragrance compounds. Listed as possible human carcinogen by IARC.
Used in powders and eyeshadows. Concerns exist around asbestos contamination in cosmetic-grade talc. Some studies link talc to ovarian cancer.
Pigment used in mascaras and eyeliners. Possible carcinogen â€” classified as Group 2B by IARC.
Microplastic film former. Does not biodegrade and accumulates in water systems. Environmental concern.
Microplastic used as exfoliant beads. Banned in rinse-off cosmetics in UK, US, and EU. Environmental pollutant.
Forever chemicals used in some foundations and waterproof cosmetics for smooth application. Linked to cancer, hormone disruption, and immune effects. Do not biodegrade.
Group of compounds used as pH adjusters and emulsifiers. Can form carcinogenic nitrosamines. DEA is the most concerning.
Silicone-based emollient that creates a smooth barrier on skin. Locks in moisture but can clog pores with prolonged use. Generally safe but avoid if acne-prone.
Volatile silicone that evaporates after application. D4 and D5 are restricted in EU wash-off products due to environmental persistence and potential endocrine effects.
Volatile silicone restricted in EU rinse-off cosmetics. Environmentally persistent.
Similar to cyclopentasiloxane. EU restricted. Persistent in the environment.
Gives hair and skin a glossy appearance. Less occlusive than dimethicone. Generally considered safe.
Conditioning silicone used in hair products. Targets damaged areas of hair. Generally safe and effective.
Film-forming silicone used in long-wear makeup. Creates a waterproof coating. Generally safe.
Lightweight silicone with good spreading properties. Generally well tolerated.
Emollient silicone that conditions skin and hair. Generally safe and non-irritating.
Heavier silicone used in conditioners and moisturisers. Generally safe.

common_aliases: Silsurf D212CG, Dow Corning 5329, DC 5329, Polyethylene Glycol-12 Dimethicone, PEG-12 Dimethicone Copolyol, Ethoxylated Dimethicone
explanation: Water-soluble silicone. PEG compounds can be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane, a potential carcinogen. Generally safe at cosmetic concentrations.
Water-soluble silicone used as a conditioning agent. Generally safe.
Water-soluble silicone emulsifier. PEG contamination concern applies.
Mineral absorbent and thickener. Completely inert and safe. Used to control shine and texture.
Variant of dimethicone with similar properties. Provides conditioning and smoothing effects. Generally safe.
Fatty alcohol derived from coconut or palm oil. Acts as an emollient and thickener. Despite the name, it is not drying and is generally well tolerated.
Blend of cetyl and stearyl alcohol. Emulsifier and emollient. Safe for most skin types including sensitive.
Fatty alcohol used as an emollient and thickener. Non-irritating and generally safe.
Long-chain fatty alcohol. Emollient and emulsifier. Very well tolerated, safe for sensitive skin.
Fatty alcohol used as an emollient. Generally safe and non-irritating.
Fatty alcohol that rates 2-3 on comedogenicity. Safe for most but may cause issues for acne-prone skin.
Fatty alcohol that can be moderately comedogenic. May irritate sensitive skin at higher concentrations.
Simple alcohol that evaporates quickly. Drying to the skin barrier with repeated use. Different from fatty alcohols.
Very drying and irritating to skin. Disrupts the lipid barrier significantly. Not suitable for facial use.
Used as a preservative and solvent. Can cause contact sensitivity and is a mild irritant. EU-regulated fragrance allergen.
Denatured ethanol. Drying to skin and disrupts the barrier. Often used in toners and astringents for a mattifying effect.
Widely used preservative considered safer than parabens. Some concerns about irritation at higher concentrations. EU limits to 1% in cosmetics.
Mild preservative booster derived from glycerin. Often paired with phenoxyethanol. Generally well tolerated.
Preservative that can form benzene (a carcinogen) when combined with vitamin C (ascorbic acid) in formulas. Generally safe alone.
Mild preservative from sorbic acid. Generally well tolerated and considered one of the safer preservatives.
Natural preservative. Gentle and generally safe. Can cause mild irritation in very sensitive individuals.
Mild preservative and skin conditioning agent. Well tolerated by most skin types.
Preservative with muscle-relaxing properties. Can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals.
Preservative that can be irritating at higher concentrations. Generally safe in rinse-off products.
Beta hydroxy acid used as both an exfoliant and preservative. Can be drying and irritating, especially for dry or sensitive skin. Avoid during pregnancy.
Mild preservative often combined with benzyl alcohol. Generally well tolerated.
Used as a solvent and preservative booster. Can be irritating at higher concentrations.
Plant-derived solvent and humectant. Gentler alternative to propylene glycol. Generally well tolerated.
Mild preservative and humectant. Derived from plant sources. Well tolerated including by sensitive skin.
Active ingredient in anti-dandruff shampoos. Effective antifungal. EU has restricted it as a leave-on cosmetic ingredient due to environmental concerns.
Anti-dandruff agent and preservative. Gentler alternative to zinc pyrithione. Generally well tolerated.
Antifungal preservative used in anti-dandruff and anti-acne products. Generally safe and effective.

common_aliases: Sodium Hyaluronate, Hyaluronan, Hyaluronate, Biohyaluronic acid, Super Hyaluronic Acid, Hydrolyzed Hyaluronic Acid, Sodium Acetylated Hyaluronate, Potassium Hyaluronate
explanation: Powerful humectant that holds up to 1000 times its weight in water. Occurs naturally in the skin. Suitable for all skin types including sensitive and acne-prone.
One of the most effective and well-studied humectants. Draws moisture from the environment into the skin. Safe for all skin types.
Effective humectant but can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals. Generally safe at concentrations under 5%.
Mild humectant and solvent. Less likely to cause irritation than propylene glycol. Generally well tolerated.

common_aliases: D-Panthenol, DL-Panthenol, Pro-Vitamin B5, Vitamin B5, Dexpanthenol
explanation: Converts to vitamin B5 in the skin. Deeply hydrating and promotes skin barrier repair. Anti-inflammatory properties. Safe for all skin types.
Natural component of the skin's Natural Moisturising Factor (NMF). Excellent humectant. Safe for all skin types.
Natural component of the skin's NMF. At low concentrations acts as a humectant. At higher concentrations acts as an exfoliant. Very effective for dry and eczema-prone skin.
Derived from sugar beets. Mild humectant and anti-irritant. Helps stabilise other ingredients in formulas.
Sugar alcohol used as a humectant. Gentle and well tolerated.
Sugar alcohol humectant. Gentle and effective. Generally well tolerated.
Natural sugar with excellent moisture-binding and protective properties. Used in hydrating products.
Vitamin-like compound with humectant and soothing properties. Beneficial for sensitive and acne-prone skin.
Amino acid that is a natural component of skin's NMF. Hydrating and gentle.
Sodium salt of lactic acid. Effective humectant and mild exfoliant. Component of the skin's NMF.
Alpha hydroxy acid derived from milk. Exfoliates by loosening the bonds between dead skin cells. Gentler than glycolic acid. Increases sun sensitivity â€” always use SPF.
Smallest AHA molecule â€” most effective but also most irritating. Exfoliates, brightens, and stimulates collagen. Increases photosensitivity significantly.
Larger AHA molecule derived from bitter almonds. Gentlest AHA. Also has antibacterial properties â€” good for acne-prone skin. Increases sun sensitivity.
Mild AHA derived from apples. Often used in combination with other AHAs. Gentle and generally well tolerated.
Mild AHA derived from grapes. Often used as a pH adjuster and mild exfoliant.
Mild AHA used primarily as a pH adjuster. At higher concentrations can exfoliate but also irritate.
Largest exfoliant molecules â€” gentlest exfoliation with additional humectant benefits. Suitable for sensitive, dry, and eczema-prone skin.
Multitasking ingredient that exfoliates, reduces hyperpigmentation, kills acne bacteria, and reduces redness. Safe during pregnancy. Excellent for rosacea.
Brightening ingredient that inhibits tyrosinase enzyme. Effective for hyperpigmentation. Can cause sensitisation with prolonged use.
Gold standard anti-ageing ingredient. Increases cell turnover, stimulates collagen, and improves texture. Can cause irritation, dryness, and photosensitivity. Avoid during pregnancy.
More potent than retinol but less so than retinoic acid. Faster results with slightly more irritation potential. Avoid during pregnancy.
Ester form of vitamin A. Weakest retinoid â€” least effective but also least irritating. Safe starting point for retinoid beginners.
Ester form of vitamin A. Milder than retinol. Converted to retinol in the skin.
Next-generation retinoid that works without conversion steps. Less irritating than retinol with effective results.

Purest form of vitamin C. Powerful antioxidant, brightens, stimulates collagen, protects against UV damage. Unstable â€” oxidises quickly. Can be irritating at high concentrations. Avoid mixing with niacinamide at high concentrations.
Stable vitamin C derivative. More gentle than ascorbic acid. Also has antibacterial properties beneficial for acne-prone skin.
Stable vitamin C derivative. Gentler and more stable than ascorbic acid. Less potent but well tolerated.
Stable, water-soluble vitamin C derivative. Gentle and effective brightener suitable for sensitive skin.

common_aliases: Vitamin C ATIP, Tetrahexyldecyl Ascorbate, VC-IP, Vitamin C Tetraisopalmitate, Ascorbyl tetra-2-hexyldecanoate
explanation: Oil-soluble, stable vitamin C derivative. Penetrates well and is very gentle. Good for dry and sensitive skin.
Stable and potent vitamin C derivative. Good skin penetration and brightening efficacy.

Multi-tasking ingredient that reduces pore appearance, controls oil, brightens skin, strengthens the barrier, and reduces redness. Well studied and safe for all skin types including acne-prone and sensitive.
Can cause temporary flushing (redness and tingling) when applied topically. Convert to niacinamide for stable benefit.
B vitamin used in hair and nail products. Supports keratin structure. Generally safe and well tolerated.
Antioxidant vitamin. Used in some serums for its skin-protective properties.
Regulates sebum production. Beneficial for oily and acne-prone skin. Anti-inflammatory properties.
Fat-soluble antioxidant that protects against oxidative damage. Works synergistically with vitamin C. Moisturising and skin-repairing.

common_aliases: Tocopherol Acetate, Vitamin E Acetate, Vitamin E
explanation: Stable esterified form of Vitamin E. Acts as an antioxidant, protects skin from free radical damage, and helps maintain skin barrier. More shelf-stable than pure Tocopherol. Safe for all skin types.
Powerful antioxidant from grape skins. Anti-inflammatory and protective against UV damage.
Plant-derived antioxidant that doubles the effectiveness of vitamins C and E. Commonly used in combination serums.
Antioxidant naturally present in skin cells. Decreases with age. Helps protect against free radical damage.
Master antioxidant naturally produced by the body. Brightening and protective. Generally safe.
One of the most powerful natural antioxidants. Anti-inflammatory and protective. Generally safe and beneficial.
Carotenoid antioxidant from tomatoes. Protective against UV damage and oxidative stress.
Antioxidant carotenoid that converts to vitamin A. Protective and brightening. Generally safe.
Potent antioxidant from green tea. Anti-inflammatory and protective against UV damage. Safe for all skin types.
Flavonoid antioxidant with anti-inflammatory and antihistamine properties. Beneficial for sensitive and reactive skin.
Key lipid in the skin barrier. Replenishes lost ceramides to restore barrier function. Essential for dry and eczema-prone skin.
Another key skin barrier ceramide. Works best in combination with other ceramides, cholesterol, and fatty acids.
One of the key ceramides found naturally in skin. Helps maintain the skin barrier, retains moisture, and protects against environmental damage. Safe for all skin types including sensitive.
Long-chain ceramide crucial for skin barrier integrity. Part of the optimal ceramide complex for barrier repair.
Essential component of the skin barrier lipid matrix alongside ceramides and fatty acids. Helps restore barrier function.

common_aliases: Linoleic Acid, Linolenic Acid, Oleic Acid
explanation: Essential fatty acids that maintain the skin barrier. Linoleic acid is particularly beneficial for acne-prone skin.

common_aliases: Perhydrosqualene, Pripure 3759, Hydrogenated Squalene, Squalane Oil
explanation: Highly stable emollient derived from olives or sugarcane. Identical to naturally occurring squalene in skin. Lightweight, non-comedogenic, and excellent for all skin types.
Naturally occurring lipid produced by sebaceous glands. Declines with age. Excellent emollient. Note: different from squalane (the stable form).
Soothing and healing agent. Promotes cell regeneration, reduces irritation, and helps repair the skin barrier. Safe for all skin types including sensitive.

common_aliases: Cica, Tiger Grass, Gotu Kola, TECA
explanation: Traditional healing plant extract. Contains madecassoside, asiaticoside, and asiatic acid. Promotes wound healing, reduces inflammation, and strengthens the skin barrier.
Active compound from centella asiatica. Potent anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties. Strengthens the skin barrier.
Active compound from centella asiatica. Stimulates collagen synthesis and promotes healing.
Polysaccharide from oats or yeast. Deeply hydrating and soothing. Stimulates collagen production. Safe for all skin types.
Anti-inflammatory and soothing. Colloidal oatmeal is an FDA-approved active ingredient for eczema relief.

common_aliases: Aloe Barbadensis Leaf Juice, Aloe Extract
explanation: Soothing, hydrating, and anti-inflammatory. Speeds wound healing. Generally safe â€” rarely some individuals are sensitive to aloe.
One of the most studied cosmetic peptides. Stimulates collagen and hyaluronic acid production. Effective anti-ageing with good safety profile.
Peptide that stimulates collagen type I synthesis. Often combined with palmitoyl tetrapeptide-7.
Peptide that reduces inflammation and glycation. Complements palmitoyl tripeptide-1 in anti-ageing formulas.
Peptide often marketed as botox-in-a-bottle. Relaxes facial tension with repeated use. Generally safe.
Promotes collagen and elastin synthesis, wound healing, and antioxidant activity. One of the most studied cosmetic peptides.
Peptide that reduces expression lines by relaxing muscular tension. Synergistic with argireline.
Synthetic peptide mimicking snake venom (temple viper). Relaxes muscle contractions to reduce wrinkles.
Stable form of arbutin that inhibits tyrosinase to reduce hyperpigmentation. Safer and more effective than beta arbutin. Safe for all skin types.
Converts to hydroquinone in the skin â€” same brightening effect but with hydroquinone-related concerns at higher concentrations.
Brightening ingredient that targets multiple pathways of pigmentation. Gentle and suitable for sensitive skin. Can be used during pregnancy.
Contains glabridin which inhibits tyrosinase. Brightening and anti-inflammatory. Gentle and safe for sensitive skin.
See Ascorbic Acid entry. Collective term for all vitamin C forms used in skincare.
Plant-derived brightening ingredient from boldo leaves. Inhibits melanin production. Well tolerated.
Natural source of arbutin. Brightening and antioxidant. Generally safe.
Contains oxyresveratrol which inhibits tyrosinase. Effective brightener with antioxidant properties.
Mineral UV filter that provides broad-spectrum UVA and UVB protection. Non-irritating and safe for sensitive, acne-prone, and reactive skin. Safe during pregnancy.
Mineral UV filter primarily for UVB protection. Non-irritating and safe for sensitive skin. Safe during pregnancy.
Photostable chemical UV filter available in EU and Asia. Not approved in US. Excellent broad-spectrum protection with low penetration.
Photostable UV filter. Hybrid mineral-chemical filter. Available in EU and Asia. Low skin penetration.
Photostable UVA filter with low penetration. Available in EU. Not approved in US.
Chamomile-derived soothing ingredient. Anti-inflammatory, skin-lightening, and wound-healing properties. Safe for sensitive and irritated skin.

common_aliases: Matricaria Chamomilla Extract, Anthemis Nobilis Flower Extract
explanation: Anti-inflammatory and soothing. Contains bisabolol and apigenin. Gentle and suitable for sensitive skin.
Anti-inflammatory and wound-healing. Rich in flavonoids and triterpenoids. Soothing for sensitive and irritated skin.
Rich in EGCG and other polyphenols. Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial. Safe for all skin types including acne-prone.
Highest antioxidant content of all tea extracts. Gentle and suitable for sensitive skin.
Potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant. Can cause temporary yellowing of skin at high concentrations.
Rich in vitamins C and A, and fatty acids. Brightening and anti-ageing. Generally safe.
Soothing and brightening. Contains glycyrrhizin which reduces inflammation. Safe for sensitive skin.
Natural source of salicylates. Mild exfoliating and antibacterial. Gentler than synthetic salicylic acid.

common_aliases: Tremella Fuciformis Extract, Shiitake Extract, Albatrellus Confluens Extract, Ganoderma Lucidum Extract, Lentinus Edodes Extract, Inonotus Obliquus Extract
explanation: Tremella mushroom holds more water than hyaluronic acid. Antioxidant and soothing. Safe for all skin types.
Rich in minerals, vitamins, and amino acids. Hydrating and antioxidant. Generally safe.
Hydrating and film-forming. Rich in minerals. Generally safe when properly formulated.
Topical collagen acts as a humectant and conditioning agent. Collagen molecules are too large to penetrate skin â€” works on the surface. Safe and non-irritating.
Hydrolyzed form acts as a humectant and conditioning agent on the skin surface. Generally safe.
Amino acid-rich proteins that condition and hydrate skin and hair. Form a protective film. Generally safe.
Structural protein of skin, hair, and nails. Hydrolyzed form strengthens hair and nails. Generally safe.
Antioxidant and mild exfoliant. Also chelates metal ions that can accelerate oxidation. Generally gentle.
PHA (polyhydroxy acid). Large molecule that exfoliates gently. Also a powerful antioxidant and humectant. Great for sensitive skin.
PHA with antioxidant and humectant properties alongside gentle exfoliation. Safe for sensitive, dry, and eczema-prone skin.
Regulates sebum production, reduces inflammation, and has antibacterial properties. Beneficial for oily and acne-prone skin.
Keratolytic and antibacterial. Effective for acne, seborrheic dermatitis, and rosacea. Can be drying and has a strong odour.

common_aliases: Selenium Sulfide, Sodium Selenite, Selenium Disulfide
explanation: Antioxidant mineral. Used in anti-dandruff products and some antioxidant serums.
Mineral pigments used in colour cosmetics and tinted SPFs. Safe and non-irritating. Also provide some UV protection.
Purine nucleoside that stimulates collagen synthesis and has anti-inflammatory properties. Good anti-ageing ingredient with an excellent safety profile.
Brightening ingredient four times more potent than kojic acid. Also has antibacterial properties. Generally well tolerated.
Stimulates cell renewal and collagen production. Controversial â€” some dermatologists concerned about potential to stimulate abnormal cell growth.

common_aliases: Plant Stem Cells, Apple Stem Cell
explanation: Plant stem cell extracts provide antioxidants and support skin's own stem cells. Generally safe but efficacy claims are often exaggerated.
Support the skin's microbiome. Anti-inflammatory and barrier-strengthening. Beneficial for acne-prone, sensitive, and eczema-prone skin.
Feed beneficial bacteria on the skin. Support microbiome balance. Generally safe and gentle.
By-products of probiotic fermentation. Anti-inflammatory and barrier-supporting. Generally well tolerated.
Contains glycoproteins, hyaluronic acid, glycolic acid, zinc, and copper. Hydrating, brightening, and healing. Generally safe.
Protein fragments that signal skin cells to produce collagen, elastin, and other proteins. Generally safe and well studied.
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory. Reduces puffiness by constricting blood vessels. Used in eye creams and anti-cellulite products. Generally safe.
Precursor to hyaluronic acid. Also has brightening properties and supports skin renewal.
Extremophile-derived molecule. Stabilises proteins and cell membranes. Protective and soothing. Excellent for sensitive and environmentally stressed skin.
Derived from algae that survive in extreme cold. Activates longevity genes in skin cells. Antioxidant and protective.
Rich in trace minerals. Used as a hydrating and mineral-replenishing ingredient. May cause irritation for some sensitive skin types.

common_aliases: Avene Thermal Spring Water, La Roche-Posay Thermal Water, Demineralized Spring Water, Avene aqua, Uriage thermal spring water, Onsen-Sui
explanation: Mineral-rich spring water with documented soothing and anti-inflammatory properties. Safe for all including sensitive skin.

common_aliases: Rosa Damascena Flower Water, Rose Hydrosol
explanation: Gentle toner with anti-inflammatory properties. Generally safe but can cause reactions in individuals sensitive to fragrances or rose.

common_aliases: Hamamelis Virginiana Bark/Leaf Extract, Alcohol-Free Witch Hazel, Hamamelis Virginiana Leaf Water, Witch Hazel Water
explanation: Alcohol-free witch hazel retains tannin-based anti-inflammatory benefits without the irritating drying effect of alcohol-based versions.

common_aliases: Nano Hyaluronic Acid, Sodium Hyaluronate Crosspolymer
explanation: Smaller HA fragments that penetrate deeper into the skin for more intensive hydration. Generally safe.
Holds 4 times more moisture than hyaluronic acid. Also inhibits the enzyme that breaks down HA, making your own HA last longer.
Topically acts as a humectant. Generally safe unless allergic to fish.
Conditioning and hydrating amino acids from silk. Safe for skin and hair.
Rich in lycopene and citrulline. Antioxidant and hydrating. Safe for all skin types.
Rich in punicalagin and ellagic acid. Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and brightening. Safe for most skin types.
Rich in vitamins, omega fatty acids, and carotenoids. Highly nutritive but can tint skin orange at high concentrations.
Antioxidant and soothing. Anti-ageing properties. Generally safe.
Potent anti-inflammatory. Can cause temporary skin yellowing and may stain clothing. Generally safe at low concentrations.

common_aliases: Retinyl Palmitate, Retinyl Acetate, Retinyl Linoleate
explanation: Ester forms of vitamin A converted to retinol in skin. Gentler than retinol but less potent. Good for beginners.
Natural mineral used to add shimmer and glow to cosmetics. Non-irritating and safe for most skin types.
Natural thickener and stabiliser. Derived from fermentation. Non-irritating and safe for all skin types.
Synthetic thickener and gel-forming agent. Generally safe and non-irritating at cosmetic concentrations.
Natural-derived thickener from cellulose. Non-irritating and safe. Used to give products a gel texture.

common_aliases: Hyaluronic Acid Sodium Salt, Kopuron, Hydrolyzed Sodium Hyaluronate, Sodium Acetylated Hyaluronate
explanation: Sodium salt of hyaluronic acid. Penetrates skin more easily than high molecular weight HA. Key hydrating ingredient.
Gentle emulsifiers derived from plant glycerin. Well tolerated by sensitive skin. Good alternative to ethoxylated emulsifiers.
Natural emulsifier from plants. Strengthens the skin barrier and has excellent hydrating properties. Generally safe.
Essential fatty acid that the body cannot produce. Deficiency linked to acne. Topical application helps restore barrier and reduce breakouts.
Stable, gentle form of vitamin C. Less irritating than ascorbic acid. Effective antioxidant and brightener.
Derived from licorice root. Potent anti-inflammatory and soothing agent. Suitable for sensitive and rosacea-prone skin.
Freshwater algae rich in proteins, vitamins, and minerals. Antioxidant and detoxifying. Generally safe.
Adaptogenic mushroom with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Supports the skin barrier.
Mineral-derived antioxidant that protects against environmental pollutants. Strengthens skin's natural defences.
Plant-derived retinol alternative from Psoralea corylifolia. Studies show comparable results to retinol with significantly less irritation. Safe during pregnancy.
Holds more water than hyaluronic acid. Traditional Asian beauty ingredient with excellent hydrating and brightening properties.
Fermented yeast extract rich in amino acids, vitamins, and minerals. Brightening and skin-strengthening.
Fermented ingredient that brightens, refines pores, and balances oil. Key ingredient in many Korean skincare products.
Fermented probiotic ingredient that strengthens the skin barrier and has anti-ageing properties.
Amino acid-derived gentle surfactant. Very mild cleanser suitable for sensitive and baby skin.

common_aliases: Cocoamidopropyl Betaine, Cocoamido propyl Betaine, CAPB, Cocoyl Amide Propyldimethyl Glycine, Cocamidopropyl Dimethyl Glycine
explanation: Amphoteric surfactant that reduces irritation of other surfactants. Generally mild but can cause contact dermatitis in some individuals.
Very gentle non-ionic surfactant derived from glucose and coconut. Suitable for sensitive and baby skin.
Gentle surfactant derived from coconut and fruit sugars. Mild and suitable for sensitive skin. Biodegradable.

common_aliases: Aqua, Water/Aqua, Purified Water, Deionized Water, Distilled Water
explanation: The base of most skincare formulations. Acts as a solvent for other ingredients. Completely safe for all skin types.

common_aliases: Zinc, L-Pyrrolidone Carboxylic Acid Zinc Salt
explanation: Zinc salt of PCA. Regulates sebum production, has antimicrobial properties. Beneficial for oily and acne-prone skin.
Emulsifier and solubilizer commonly used in toners and mists. Generally safe but can cause irritation in sensitive individuals with prolonged use.
Chemical UV filter used in sunscreens. Can cause contact allergies in sensitive individuals. May degrade into benzophenone over time which is a potential endocrine disruptor.
Chemical UV filter used in sunscreens. Weak UVB absorber. Can cause irritation in sensitive skin. Avoid if you have salicylate sensitivity.
Chelating agent that binds metal ions and prevents product spoilage. Generally safe in rinse-off products. Can enhance penetration of other ingredients — concern if used with harmful actives.
Gentle plant-derived surfactant made from coconut oil and fruit sugars. One of the mildest cleansing agents available. Safe for all skin types including sensitive and baby skin.
Humectant and solvent that helps other ingredients penetrate skin. Enhances absorption of actives. Generally well tolerated but can cause mild irritation at high concentrations.
Amino acid based surfactant derived from coconut oil and glycine. Very gentle cleanser that maintains skin's natural pH. Suitable for sensitive and dry skin.

common_aliases: Camellia Sinensis Extract, Green Tea Extract, White Tea Extract, Camellia Oleifera Extract
explanation: Antioxidant rich plant extract from the tea plant. Protects against free radical damage, soothes inflammation, and has mild antimicrobial properties. Safe for all skin types.

common_aliases: Sweet Almond Oil, Prunus Amygdalus Dulcis Oil, Prunus Dulcis Oil
explanation: Emollient oil rich in fatty acids and Vitamin E. Moisturising and soothing. Can clog pores for acne-prone skin. Avoid if you have a nut allergy.

common_aliases: Colloidal Oatmeal, Avena Sativa Extract, Oat Extract, Avena Sativa Kernel Extract
explanation: Soothing and anti-inflammatory extract from oats. Relieves itching, reduces redness, and strengthens the skin barrier. Excellent for sensitive, dry, and eczema-prone skin.

common_aliases: Caprylic Capric Triglyceride, MCT Oil, Fractionated Coconut Oil, C8-C10 Triglycerides
explanation: Lightweight emollient derived from coconut oil. Excellent skin feel, non-greasy, and helps other ingredients absorb. Safe for all skin types including acne-prone.
Plant-derived emulsifier system from olive oil. Mimics skin's natural lipid structure, enhances moisture retention. Exceptionally gentle and suitable for all skin types.
Lightweight emollient derived from coconut oil. Provides a dry, silky skin feel without greasiness. Non-comedogenic and suitable for all skin types.

common_aliases: Trisodium Citrate, Citric Acid Sodium Salt
explanation: pH adjuster and buffering agent derived from citric acid. Helps maintain product stability and skin's natural pH. Safe for all skin types.

common_aliases: Simulgel NS, Aristoflex AVC, Sodium Acryloyldimethyl Taurate Copolymer
explanation: Synthetic polymer used as a thickener and emulsifier. Creates a lightweight gel texture. Non-comedogenic and well tolerated by all skin types including sensitive.

common_aliases: Acrylic Acid Copolymer, Acrylates/C10-30 Alkyl Acrylate Crosspolymer
explanation: Synthetic polymer used as a film former and thickener. Helps products spread evenly on skin. Generally safe but may cause irritation in very sensitive individuals.
Synthetic film-forming polymer. Used to improve texture and wear of products. Limited safety data available. Some concerns around styrene as a potential irritant at high concentrations.
Synthetic polymer used as a film former and thickener in sunscreens. Helps create water resistant formulations. Considered safe at concentrations used in cosmetics.

Synthetic polymer used as a texture enhancer and slip agent. Creates a smooth skin feel. Microplastic concern — environmental issue. Generally safe for skin but avoid if concerned about microplastics.

common_aliases: Cassava Starch, Manihot Esculenta Root Starch
explanation: Natural thickener and absorbent derived from cassava root. Gives products a silky, powdery finish. Absorbs excess oil. Safe for all skin types.
Biomimetic humectant derived from plant sugars. Binds to skin keratin and provides long-lasting hydration up to 72 hours. Suitable for all skin types including sensitive.
Fatty alcohol used as emollient and thickener. Gives products a rich texture. Can be comedogenic for acne-prone skin at high concentrations.

common_aliases: Oryza Sativa Extract, Oryza Sativa (Rice) Extract, Rice Bran Extract
explanation: Antioxidant-rich extract from rice. Brightens skin tone, reduces hyperpigmentation, and provides mild UV protection. Safe for all skin types.

common_aliases: Sake, Fermented Rice Water, Rice Ferment Filtrate (Sake), Pitera
explanation: Fermented rice water rich in vitamins, minerals, and amino acids. Brightens skin, improves texture, and boosts hydration. Safe for all skin types.

common_aliases: Nelumbo Nucifera Extract, Lotus Extract, Sacred Lotus Extract
explanation: Antioxidant and soothing extract from the white lotus flower. Helps brighten skin and reduce inflammation. Safe for all skin types.
Lightweight synthetic emollient. Restores skin barrier function and provides excellent skin feel without greasiness. Non-comedogenic and suitable for all skin types.
Lightweight emollient ester. Provides a dry, non-greasy skin feel. Enhances spreadability of products. Non-comedogenic and suitable for all skin types.
Lightweight hydrocarbon emollient. Provides a clean, non-greasy skin feel. Biodegradable alternative to silicones. Safe for all skin types including acne-prone.
PEG-based emulsifier. Generally safe but may be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane during manufacturing — a potential carcinogen. Avoid if sensitive or concerned about PEG compounds.
Lightweight synthetic emollient ester. Provides a silky, non-greasy skin feel. Non-comedogenic and suitable for all skin types including oily and acne-prone.
PEG-modified olive oil used as emollient and emulsifier. Gentle and moisturising. Contains PEG which may cause sensitivity in some individuals.
Biodegradable chelating agent used as a greener alternative to EDTA. Prevents product oxidation and extends shelf life. Safe for all skin types.

common_aliases: Tego Amid S 18, Amino Acid Surfactant
explanation: Mild sugar-based surfactant. Gentle cleanser that maintains skin's natural pH and moisture levels. Suitable for sensitive and dry skin.
Mild amino acid based surfactant blend. Gentle cleansing with good foam and skin feel. Safe for sensitive and dry skin types.

common_aliases: Retinoic Acid, All-Trans Retinoic Acid, Vitamin A Acid
explanation: Prescription-strength retinoid. Most effective retinoid for anti-aging and acne but causes significant irritation, dryness, and purging especially in early use. Avoid during pregnancy. Always use SPF when using tretinoin.
Third generation retinoid. More stable and less irritating than tretinoin. Effective for acne and early anti-aging. Available OTC in some countries. Avoid during pregnancy.

Powerful antibacterial agent that kills acne-causing bacteria. Very effective for inflammatory acne but causes significant dryness, irritation, and bleaches fabric. Start with low concentrations (2.5%).
Antimicrobial agent used in some acne treatments and cleansers. Effective against bacteria but can disrupt skin microbiome with prolonged use. Can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals.

common_aliases: Vitamin C Ester, Ascorbic Acid Palmitate
explanation: Fat-soluble form of Vitamin C. More stable than ascorbic acid. Antioxidant that protects skin from free radical damage. Less potent than pure Vitamin C but gentler and more stable.
Synthetic antioxidant considered more powerful than Vitamin E and CoQ10. Protects against environmental damage and reduces signs of aging. Well tolerated by most skin types.
Naturally occurring enzyme and powerful antioxidant. Neutralizes free radicals and protects against oxidative stress. Supports skin repair and reduces inflammation.
